frameworks.name: neural variability quenching
frameworks.one_line: Post-stimulus reduction of trial-to-trial response variability

frameworks.name: prestimulus-poststimulus interaction
frameworks.one_line: Ongoing prestimulus dynamics shape evoked poststimulus activity

frameworks.name: trial-to-trial variability analysis
frameworks.one_line: Uses TTV as the dependent measure across frequency bands

core_question: How do prestimulus dynamics shape poststimulus trial-to-trial variability quenching?
key_assumptions: Standard deviation across trials operationalizes prestimulus variability
Stimulus-evoked activity is separable from ongoing dynamics
Variability quenching is a meaningful index of stimulus processing
manipulations: Split trials by high vs low prestimulus variability
Compared early (0-300ms) vs later (300-600ms) poststimulus windows
Frequency band analyses including theta and alpha
Replication across sEEG and scalp EEG datasets
key_findings: Greater variability quenching in trials with high prestimulus variability across all frequency bands
Relative stimulus effect was larger in the later than the earlier poststimulus window
High prestimulus theta and alpha variability predicted faster reaction times
Findings replicated across intracranial sEEG and EEG datasets
interpretations: Stimulus-related activity is a blend of external stimulus effects and ongoing prestimulus dynamics
Prestimulus variability spills over into and shapes poststimulus responses and behavior
